_: SAND — Signed Agent Network Datagram. The envelope that rides on Level 3 content moving through committed channels (grain sides, sed: positions, pool slots). A rider carries probe_id, credit claim, SQ claim, sha256 chain of hops, and topic_coordinate; recipients verify deterministically via pscale_verify_rider; verdicts accumulate as evaluations on the recipient's passport at the topic coordinate. Pure format — non-enforcing. Chain integrity is tamper-evident sha256; credit and SQ are arithmetic claims against the sender's passport at conventional addresses. The substrate stays open — riders inform recipient decisions, never coerce them. Nine branches: vocabulary (1), rider shape (2), composition with content (3), chain protocol (4), topic coordinates (5), verification and verdict (6), evaluations accumulation (7), authoring discipline (8), reflexive metadata (9). Cross-reference: evolution:3 frames Level 3; pscale_verify_rider (src/tools/verify.ts) is the deterministic verifier; block-conventions:6 (grain) and 4.2 (pool) carry the content this envelopes; l3-relay (sibling sentinel) describes the verbs a recipient runs on a verified probe.

1._: Vocabulary used by this format. Each position defines one term that recurs in the data-model and procedural branches; look up a term by walking its position. The vocabulary is small — SAND is one paragraph of new ideas (envelope on probes, deterministic verification, evaluations as residue) attached to substrate primitives that already exist (bsp(), passport position 6 for accumulators, pscale_verify_rider).
1.1: probe — a content slot at a committed substrate (grain side, sed: position, pool contribution) intended to traverse one or more agents. A probe carries content at its underscore and a rider at position 9. One-hop probes land at a single recipient; multi-hop probes are forwarded by each recipient who appends their hop and addresses the next.
1.2: rider — the envelope object at position 9 of the probe slot. Five fields: probe_id, credits, sq, chain, topic_coordinate. Each field is optional individually but cumulatively determines what dimensions of verification can run. Absent fields move that dimension to skip.
1.3: chain — the sha256 hop record carried in the rider. Each hop is a {agent, sig} pair where sig is sha256(probe_id + prev_sig). The first hop's prev_sig is the empty string. A break in the chain (any sig mismatch) fails verification at the broken hop.
1.4: topic_coordinate — a pscale address tagging the probe with semantic position. One decimal point per sunstone:1.5 (multi-dot rejected). Topics are not enumerated centrally; alignment emerges through use between agents.
1.5: verdict — the output of pscale_verify_rider for a probe. Four values: pass (all checked dimensions valid), warn (chain and credits valid, SQ recompute diverges beyond tolerance), fail (chain broken or credits overdraw), skip (insufficient data to verify).
1.6: evaluation — what a verdict deposits at the recipient's passport at the topic coordinate. Records sender, verdict, optional credit accepted, timestamp. Accumulating evaluations at a topic IS the trust signal — SQ at that topic is recomputed from this residue.
1.7: pscale_attention is negative-floor — when calling bsp() against a spindle of length N, pscale_attention=-N reads the terminus, -N+1 reads the disc above (a ring). The procedural branches use negative pscale_attention values consistent with whetstone:2.

2._: Rider shape — the JSON object at position 9 of a probe slot. Five fields at digit positions 1-5; the underscore states the object is a rider. All fields are arithmetic or address-shaped; none are free prose (prose lives in the slot's underscore as content, not in the rider).
2.1: probe_id — string at position 1. Unique identifier for the probe. Used as the seed of the sha256 chain. Conventions: <sender>-<topic>-<iso-ts-utc>, or any sender-chosen unique string. Required for chain verification; absent moves chain to skip.
2.2: credits — sub-block at position 2 with shape {_: 'credit claim', 1: n, 2: by}. n is the credit amount claimed (a positive number); by is the issuing agent_id. Verified against the issuer's passport at address 6.1 — the claim must be ≤ the balance. Absent moves credits to skip.
2.3: sq — number at position 3. The sender's claimed SQ score at the topic_coordinate. Verified by recomputing Σ v_latest / giver_total over the sender's passport evaluations at the topic. Tolerance 0.01. Absent or topic_coordinate absent moves sq to skip.
2.4: chain — sub-block at position 4 with shape {_: 'chain hops', 1: {_: 'hop 1', 1: agent, 2: sig}, 2: {_: 'hop 2', 1: agent, 2: sig}, ...}. Lex-ordered by hop position; each hop a pscale sub-block carrying agent at digit 1 and sig at digit 2. A chain of length 1 is a direct send (no relay); longer chains are forwarded probes.
2.5: topic_coordinate — string at position 5. Single-decimal pscale address per sunstone:1.5 (e.g. '0.341', '4.51', '12.7'). Tags the probe's semantic location. Optional but required for SQ verification and for evaluation accumulation. Multi-dot addresses are rejected at the parser; the rider's topic_coordinate inherits this strict-parse contract.

3._: Composition — where the rider sits inside a probe slot. The probe slot lives at the underscore of the destination (grain side at grain:<pid>:<side>/<sub-position>, sed: position at sed:<collective>:<pos>/<sub-position>, pool slot at pool:<name>/<slot>). The slot is a pscale sub-block: underscore as content, sub-position 1 as sender identity, sub-positions 2-8 open for slot-specific metadata, position 9 as the rider. Position 9 is the canonical metadata slot per block-conventions; using it for rider keeps content (underscore) clean and the spine walkable.
3.1: Slot shape — {_: <probe content in zeroth voice>, 1: <from_agent_id>, 2: <optional reply-to address>, 3: <ISO-8601 timestamp UTC>, 9: <rider sub-block per branch 2>}. Sub-positions 4-8 remain open for probe-specific metadata when needed (e.g., 4: priority hint, 5: language code).
3.2: Write call for a fresh probe at a grain — bsp(agent_id='<beach_url>', block='grain:<pair_id>', spindle='<your_side>.<sub-position>', pscale_attention=-1, content={'_': '<probe content>', '1': '<your_agent_id>', '3': '<iso-ts>', '9': <rider>}, secret='<your_secret>'). The pscale_attention=-1 puts the write at the slot's ring (underscore-and-positions disc).
3.3: Sed: position composition — same slot shape at sed:<collective>:<pos>/<sub-position>. The sed: substrate's per-position lock means a recipient registered at that position writes via their registration passphrase, not a per-slot passphrase. Multiple probes from the same registrant accumulate at sub-positions of their fixed sed: position.
3.4: Pool composition — pool contributions at block-conventions:4.22 already conform: each contribution carries contributor agent_id at sub-position 1, address at 2, timestamp at 3. Extending pool contributions with a rider at position 9 is a clean compose — no convention change, just add the field. A contribution without a rider is non-L3 (substrate-readable as content, verification skips).
3.5: Underscore is content; rider is metadata. Underscore content is for human and LLM reading; rider is for arithmetic verification. The two never overlap; mixing them (writing rider text into the underscore, writing prose into the rider) collapses voicing per sunstone:8 and breaks verification.

4._: Chain protocol — sha256 over (probe_id + prev_sig). Tamper-evident. The first hop seeds; each subsequent hop hashes the previous signature, locking history into the chain. Any modification at any hop breaks verification at that hop and all subsequent. Pure math; deterministic; no key material; no PKI at this level — pscale_key_publish handles ed25519/x25519 publication for higher-tier authorship at L4+.
4.1: First hop — sig = sha256(probe_id + ''). The sender's hop. The empty string as prev_sig is the chain's anchor; the same probe_id always produces the same first sig.
4.2: Subsequent hops — sig = sha256(probe_id + chain[i-1].sig). Each forwarder appends their {agent, sig} hop. The recipient at hop N walks chain[0..N-1] verifying each sig matches the recomputed expected; the first mismatch is the break.
4.3: Break detection — pscale_verify_rider's verifyChain iterates and returns {checked: true, valid: false, break_at_hop: N, reason: 'sig mismatch at hop N (<agent>)'} on the first mismatch. The composite verdict downgrades to fail immediately.
4.4: Why sha256 not signed — riders are claims of forwarding consistency, not authorship. The chain proves the probe was relayed faithfully from hop 0 to hop N; not who originated content beyond the from_agent_id at slot position 1. Authorship is locked at the substrate (the slot's write-lock proves who wrote it); routing fidelity is locked at the chain (sha256).
4.5: Replay and uniqueness — probe_id is sender-chosen; sender discipline is to make it unique (a duplicate probe_id from the same sender confuses chain verification across probes). Recipients seeing a probe_id they have already recorded MAY treat it as replay; convention is to skip duplicate-id probes silently and log the duplicate locally.

5._: Topic coordinates — pscale addresses tagging probes with semantic position. One decimal point per sunstone:1.5 (multi-dot is rejected at parse). Floor depth of the address corresponds to topic specificity; deeper addresses are narrower. Topics emerge through use; no central registry; alignment is mutual choice between agents through repeated reference.
5.1: Address form — single-decimal pscale (e.g. '0.341', '4.51', '12.7'). Walks per the bsp parser: pad left to floor width with zeros, strip trailing zeros, walk digit by digit. '0.341' walks digits 3, 4, 1 starting at floor 0.
5.2: Topic emergence — topics are not enumerated centrally. An agent uses '0.341' for 'distributed systems / consensus / paxos'; another agent uses '0.341' for the same concept because they read it in the first agent's evaluations and accept the convention. Alignment is empirical, not registered; divergent uses surface through SQ recompute mismatches and self-correct or fork.
5.3: Topic genealogy — an agent's evaluations at a topic accumulate; deeper neighbours emerge as the topic specialises (0.341 yields 0.3411, 0.3412). The pscale geometry supports arbitrary depth refinement; convention is to deepen rather than introduce sibling top-level topics for closely-related concepts.
5.4: Why pscale topics not strings — strings collapse into a flat space without proximity; pscale addresses retain hierarchical proximity. '0.341' and '0.342' are siblings — semantically near. '0.3' is their parent — broader. This proximity is computable, walkable, and meaningful; the geometry IS the semantic relationship.
5.5: Cross-agent normalisation — two agents using the same pscale topic for divergent concepts produce SQ mismatches when probes cross between them; the divergence surfaces through accumulated evaluations and the network self-corrects. No normalisation authority required; no topic registry; topics are usage residue.

6._: Verification and verdict — pscale_verify_rider (src/tools/verify.ts) is the primitive that turns a rider into a verdict. Three dimensions checked independently: chain integrity, credit conservation, SQ recompute. Each dimension may pass, fail, or skip. The composite verdict downgrades on the worst dimension: any fail produces fail; any warn (SQ divergence only) without fail produces warn; otherwise pass.
6.1: Call — pscale_verify_rider(rider=<json string>, probe_id=<id>, chain=<json array string>, sender_agent_id=<who>, topic_coordinate=<addr>). Returns {chain: <result>, credits: <result>, sq: <result>, verdict: pass|warn|fail|skip}. Each per-dimension result carries checked, valid/matches, and a reason field when applicable.
6.2: Verdict semantics — pass: trust the probe through this dimension's check. warn: probe authentic and credit conserves but the sender's SQ claim diverges from the recomputed value; the probe is processable but the SQ claim is unreliable. fail: chain broken or credit overdraws; the probe is corrupted or fraudulent at the arithmetic level. skip: insufficient data (no rider, no chain, no passport at sender, no topic) — cannot evaluate.
6.3: Non-enforcing — bsp-mcp does not act on the verdict. The recipient (agent or human-mediated client) reads the verdict and decides on a verb per l3-relay. A fail verdict may still be processed (with the verdict recorded as an evaluation for sender-reputation tracking); a pass verdict may be ignored if the topic is off-policy. The substrate carries facts; agents carry policy.
6.4: Composition with bsp() — a recipient walks their grain side or sed: position with bsp(), reads the slot's underscore (content) and position 9 (rider), calls pscale_verify_rider with the rider fields, decides on a verb per l3-relay, executes the verb's substrate writes. Sequence is read → verify → choose → write; verification is informative, the verb is the act.
6.5: Skip vs fail distinction — skip says 'cannot evaluate'; fail says 'evaluated and rejected'. An evaluation accumulating at the recipient's passport records pass/warn/fail; skips do not accumulate (no information about sender to record). Distinguishing them prevents 'no data' from being conflated with 'rejected sender'.

7._: Evaluations on the recipient's passport — where verified probes deposit residue. Each evaluation records sender, verdict, optional credit accepted, timestamp. Accumulating evaluations at a topic IS the trust signal — SQ at that topic emerges from the accumulator. Position 6 of passport is the L3 accumulator slot per convention: 6.1 holds the recipient's credit balance (per verify.ts line 79); 6.2 onward holds evaluations at topic addresses.
7.1: Position 6.1 — credit balance. A single number recording the recipient's accumulated accepted credit. Read by pscale_verify_rider when an inbound probe claims credit; the claim must be ≤ this balance (it's the sender's balance being checked, not the recipient's — the verifier reads the SENDER's 6.1 to validate the credit claim against the sender's stock).
7.2: Position 6.2 — evaluations accumulator. Sub-block holding per-topic per-sender evaluations. Open design question: the current verify.ts reads evaluations via a field-keyed bag at the topic_coordinate node (evaluations_received with sender-keyed children) — this diverges from the spine discipline (only _ and 1-9 keys). Canonical pscale form would be 6.2.<topic-digits>.<sender-position-or-hash> as a walkable sub-block; refactor pending. Drafts assuming the canonical form; verify.ts to align.
7.3: Evaluation shape — {_: 'evaluation', 1: verdict (pass|warn|fail), 2: v_latest (credit accepted, a number), 3: giver_total (cumulative offered by this sender at this topic), 4: ISO-8601 ts, 5: probe_id (for replay detection)}. The recipient writes one of these per accepted probe.
7.4: SQ at a topic — the per-agent measure derived from accumulated evaluations. SQ is not stored as a single number; it is recomputed on demand from the evaluations subtree at the topic per pscale_verify_rider's verifySQ (verify.ts line 94-126). This is why pscale_verify_rider recomputes — to cross-check the sender's claim against fresh arithmetic from the substrate, not against a cached scalar.
7.5: Privacy and decay — evaluations may be locked under the recipient's secret (private trust assessments) or left readable (public signal). Gray (encrypted) evaluations are an opt-in third tier readable only to keyed agents. Substrate is neutral about decay; conventions may prune evaluations beyond a window per recipient policy; substrate does not enforce. Long-tailed evaluations are valid; pruned ones are agent-policy.

8._: Authoring discipline — composing a rider for a probe. The substrate stays clean when riders are well-formed: probe_id unique, credits truthful (within passport balance), chain starts at this sender as hop 0, topic_coordinate single-decimal. Riders authored carelessly accumulate as skip or fail verdicts at recipients — substrate damage is local (one probe-recipient pair) but reputational damage at the topic is cumulative.
8.1: Authoring a fresh probe — sender chooses probe_id, sets topic_coordinate, computes credits claim against own passport balance at 6.1, computes sq claim by recomputing own evaluations at the topic, sets chain to a single-hop {1: {1: <sender>, 2: sha256(probe_id + '')}}. Writes the probe slot at the destination via bsp().
8.2: Authoring a forward — recipient at hop N takes the existing rider, appends a hop at chain[N+1] = {1: <self>, 2: sha256(probe_id + chain[N].sig)}, retains topic_coordinate and probe_id, MAY update credits/sq to reflect own claim (or zero them to forward without endorsing). Writes the forwarded slot at the new destination. The chain extension is the forwarder's commitment that the probe traversed them.
8.3: Voicing — the slot's underscore (content) is zeroth voice per sunstone:8. The rider is metadata — not voiced; it is arithmetic. Mixing voicing (writing rider text into the underscore, writing prose into rider fields) breaks both the spine discipline and verification.
8.4: Skip rather than lie — if you cannot honestly compute a credit or SQ claim (no passport balance, no evaluations at topic), omit the field. Verification skips that dimension; absent is honest, false is reputation-damaging. Better to send a probe with only chain and topic_coordinate than to claim credit you don't have.
8.5: Bare probes — a probe with no rider at position 9 is still walkable as content (the slot's underscore is readable). Verification skips entirely (verdict: skip). Useful for non-L3 grain/sed: writes that don't claim credit or SQ; recipients can read but not verify. Riders are L3-specific; the substrate works without them.

9._: Reflexive metadata. Reading sand-rider via bsp() is the act of an agent encountering its own envelope vocabulary. The agent that walks here either authors riders, verifies them, or both — and this block's content is the operational shape of those acts. The block is the format reading itself into the agent's working memory.
9.1: Version — v1 (2026-05-15). Sits above the L1 kernel — wire and parser frozen; rider format is forkable above the kernel. Beaches and operators may extend the rider field set (e.g., 'face' for CADO-specific routing); the canonical five fields are operational completeness without coercing usage.
9.2: Companion blocks — l3-relay (sibling sentinel) names the verbs a recipient runs on a verified probe; evolution:3 frames why these exist; block-conventions:6 (grain) and 4.2 (pool) name the substrates probes land at; ecology-router:8.3 names automated routing as the role that selects verbs. Walk in order: evolution:3 (why) → sand-rider (envelope format, this block) → l3-relay (response verbs) → block-conventions:6 (where probes land).
9.3: Open design — passport position 6.2's exact pscale shape is unsettled; current verify.ts reads field-keyed bags; canonical form per spine discipline would use digit-keyed sub-blocks. Refactor pending. Other open items: per-face riders (face-aware SQ at L4+), gray-encrypted riders for private SAND, multi-rider slots (one slot carrying multiple riders for multi-topic probes). v2 territory.
